policy_statement: Implement an integrated tolling system for bridges/freeways and on Treasure Island to prioritize transit and higher-occupancy vehicles (TLU.3-4).
mechanism_description: It develops and implements integrated bridge/freeway tolling that prioritizes transit and higher-occupancy vehicles, encouraging shifts away from single-occupancy driving and lowering transportation emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Travel pricing + lane/toll priority → mode shift + ↓ VMT/idle time → ↓ gasoline/diesel combustion → ↓ transport CO₂e emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Implements tolls/cordon or corridor pricing and/or grants priority (priced lanes, HOV/HOT rules, transit priority) that makes single-occupancy vehicle travel more expensive and/or slower relative to carpools and transit.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Behavioral response reduces car trip demand, increases vehicle occupancy, shifts trips to transit/active modes, and can reduce stop-and-go congestion—lowering fuel consumed per passenger-km and total fuel burned.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced combustion of transport fuels decreases CO₂ and other GHG emissions (with possible co-benefits from reduced air pollutants).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing on-road transport GHG emissions by shifting trips away from single-occupancy driving (and often reducing VMT and congestion-related fuel use).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Pricing and priority measures primarily reduce fossil-fuel use in road transport via demand management (mode shift, higher occupancy, reduced VMT/congestion) → lower GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While these policies can improve traffic efficiency or generate revenue, the largest climate-relevant effect—when present—is emissions reduction from reduced driving/fuel combustion, which is Mitigation rather than Resource Efficiency (energy savings are not the main framing across all instances) or Adaptation (not hazard-focused) or Nature-Based Solutions.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific scheme mainly reallocates road space without reducing VMT (or induces demand elsewhere), mitigation benefits may be smaller; if revenues are the main goal, climate impact is still typically via reduced driving. These do not change the dominant mechanism classification for the set.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that uses road pricing and HOV/transit priority to deter single-occupancy driving shares the same primary mitigation pathway of reducing transport emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether a given policy’s main effect is mode shift vs congestion smoothing vs revenue-funded transit improvements depends on design and local context and should be assigned at the individual policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Possible rebound/traffic diversion effects can reduce net GHG benefits; some implementations may primarily fund transit (indirect mitigation) or optimize flow (smaller emission reductions), but the canonical mechanism remains transport emissions reduction via demand management.
key_indicators: "implement an integrated system of tolling"; "tolling for bridges and freeways"; "on Treasure Island"; "prioritize transit"; "higher occupancy vehicles"
co_benefits: Reduced congestion/travel delay; improved local air quality (criteria pollutants); potential revenue for transit; reduced noise.
instance_edge_case_notes: While the text includes "prioritize transit and higher occupancy vehicles" (could also involve operational/regulatory lane management), the central instrument explicitly specified is tolling/pricing, so it is classified as a pricing mechanism.
